coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of periodate-hydrogen peroxide chemiluminescence by nitrogen doped carbon dots and its application for the determination of pyrogallol and gallic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Shah, Syed Niaz Ali
Li, Haifang
Lin, Jin-Ming
coredata.dc:description: A new sensitized chemiluminescence (CL) was developed to broaden the analytical application of KIO4–H2O2 system. The nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) dramatically boosted the CL intensity of KIO4–H2O2 system which was further enriched by basic medium. In light of EPR analysis, free radical scavenging studies and CL spectra the detail mechanism for the enhancement was conferred in the presence of N-CDs and NaOH. The results suggested that CL of KIO4–H2O2 system in the presence and absence of N-CDs and NaOH proceeds via radical pathway. The enhanced CL was used for the determination of pyrogallol and gallic acid in range of 1.0×10−4–1.0×10−7 M with 4.6×10−8 and 6.1×10−8 M limit of detection respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) at a concentration of 10−5 for gallic acid and pyrogallol was 1.4% and 2.3% respectively (n=11). The attained results unveil that the present method is sensitive, faster, simpler and less costly compared to other methods and could be applied to determine polyphenols in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Enhancement
Mechanism
Pyrogallol
Gallic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calibration curves and detection limit of CL oxidation of pyrogallol and gallic acid

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81373373 , 91213305 and 21227006 ). In addition, S.N.A Shah highly acknowledges the Chinese Scholarship Council for the award of doctoral studies. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM image of the water soluble N-CDs. A single N-CD magnified in the inset. The corresponding size histograms are given aside.

The PL spectra of N-CDs: Black; Excitation spectrum at a maximum emission of 366nm, Different colors; emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. The emission intensity increases from 240 to 260nm and beyond that decreases till 300nm, after that increases again till a maximum emission of 366nm and again decreases till 440nm.

The mechanism of enhancement for the CL of KIO4–H2O2 system by N-CDs and the decreasing and increasing effect by acids and base, respectively. The KIO4 was 0.01M, while H2O2, HCl, N-CDs and NaOH were 0.1M. The volume of all reagents was 50µL. The negative voltage of photomultiplier tube was 1300V and time interval was set for 0.1s.

CL curves of KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs system with different mixing orders: (1) H2O2 injected into the mixture of KIO4 and N-CDs; (2) N-CDs injected into the mixture of KIO4 and H2O2; (3) KIO4 injected into the mixture of H2O2 and N-CDs. The KIO4 was 0.001M while H2O2 was 0.01M and the volume of all reagents were 50μL. The negative voltage of photomultiplier tube was 1300V and time interval was set for 0.1s.

EPR spectra of (A) nitroxide radicals provoked by the reaction of TEMP and 1O2 and (B) DMPO/•OH and DMPO/•O2 − adducts in (i) KIO4–H2O2 system and (ii) KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs system (iii) KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs–NaOH system (iv) KIO4–N-CDs system. Conditions: KIO4 and NaOH were 0.01M, H2O2, N-CDs and DMPO were 0.1M while TEMP was 10% by volume. All the solutions were prepared in water.

CL spectra of KIO4–H2O2 system in the presence and absence of N-CDs and NaOH: (a) KIO4–H2O2, (b) KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs, (c) KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs–NaOH. The concentration of KIO4 was 0.01M while that of H2O2, N-CDs and NaOH was 0.1M. The flow rate of each reagent was 2mL/min.
Reactions of pyrogallol and gallic acid with superoxide ion. They consume the superoxide ion, and results in the decrease of CL intensity in a linear range of 1.0×10−4–1.0×10−7 M.

Linear relationship between net CL counts and concentration of pyrogallol and gallic acid. Range: 1.0×10−4–1.0×10−7 M. The Pearson's correlation coefficient (R) was 0.996 for pyrogallol and 0.993 for gallic acid. Each result is an average of triplicate readings. The concentration of KIO4 was 0.001M while that of H2O2 and N-CDs was 0.1M. The volume of each solution was 50µL.
Effect of free radical scavengers with and without the presence of N-CDs and NaOH.
Analytical parameters for the determination of pyrogallol and gallic acid.

head.ce:title: Enhancement of periodate-hydrogen peroxide chemiluminescence by nitrogen doped carbon dots and its application for the determination of pyrogallol and gallic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Microanalytical Methods and Instrumentation, the Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Microanalytical Methods and Instrumentation, the Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Functionalized Probes for Chemical Imaging in University of Shandong, Shandong Normal University, Jinan 250014, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Functionalized Probes for Chemical Imaging in University of Shandong, Shandong Normal University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Beijing Key Laboratory of Microanalytical Methods and Instrumentation, the Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Microanalytical Methods and Instrumentation, the Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new sensitized chemiluminescence (CL) was developed to broaden the analytical application of KIO4–H2O2 system. The nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) dramatically boosted the CL intensity of KIO4–H2O2 system which was further enriched by basic medium. In light of EPR analysis, free radical scavenging studies and CL spectra the detail mechanism for the enhancement was conferred in the presence of N-CDs and NaOH. The results suggested that CL of KIO4–H2O2 system in the presence and absence of N-CDs and NaOH proceeds via radical pathway. The enhanced CL was used for the determination of pyrogallol and gallic acid in range of 1.0×10−4–1.0×10−7 M with 4.6×10−8 and 6.1×10−8 M limit of detection respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) at a concentration of 10−5 for gallic acid and pyrogallol was 1.4% and 2.3% respectively (n=11). The attained results unveil that the present method is sensitive, faster, simpler and less costly compared to other methods and could be applied to determine polyphenols in real samples.

The mechanism of the N-CDs-enhanced KIO4–H2O2 CL was revealed as decomposition-assisted catalytic effect along with chemical resonance energy transfer (CRET) and electron and holes induction and annihilation process, based on studies of CL emission spectra, electron spin resonance spectra, UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectra and free radical scavenging studies.

ce:section.ce:para: The enhancement, mechanism and potential applications of ultra-weak CL being new arising systems have gradually established research interest recently. Among them peroxide induced ultra-weak CL systems are the most important, extensively used and widely investigated one. Additionally, the periodate (IO4 −) being colorless, avoids emission absorption problems [1], produces soft oxidation products and are of paramount importance in micro-analysis of great number of inorganic and organic compounds [2]. The IO4 − react with H2O2 in acidic, neutral and basic mediums to give IO3 − and 1O2 [3], produces weak CL which were enhanced by some researcher [4], and used for the determination of certain compounds [5,6]. Similarly, the CL engendered by luminol–KIO4 system in basic medium was enhanced [7–9] or inhibited [10] by some compounds and analytical methods have been developed founded on such vicissitudes [8–10]. Furthermore, the inhibition or enhancement of different organic compounds were linked to pH, number and position of –OH and –NH2 functional groups as well as to the stability of free radicals generated in luminol–KIO4–H2O2 CL reaction [11]. In addition, KIO4 oxidizes organic compounds with vicinal –OH, –CHO, =CO, or –COOH groups, to aldehydes or ketones according to their structures [12]. Similarly, the oxidation of some polyhydric phenols like pyrogallol and gallic acid with H2O2 or IO4 − was testified to give weak CL, with excited 1O2 as emitter [5].

Generally, most weak CL enhancement by QDs (heavy-metal quantum dots) was brought about by an energy transfer process and is of great significance to explore the behavior of QDs for developing novel CL sensors. As, Zhou et al. demonstrated the enhancement of NaClO–H2O2 CL, to the catalysis of QDs, to decompose H2O2, producing 1O2 and (1O2)2, which by giving its energy to QDs resulted in enhanced CL [13]. These QDs, however, have raised environmental concerns, over potential toxicity and cost-effectiveness. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop simple, environmentally friendly and low-cost methods to synthesize carbon dots (CDs). CDs allocation the features of QDs, like good photo-stability and solubility in water. In tallying, CDs possess chemical stability, good biocompatibility and low toxicity, permit them to be best substitute for QDs [14,15]. However, surface-passivation is necessary to produce CDs with adequate quantum yields (QYs). Otherwise, highly photoluminescent (PL) CDs could be produced using nitrogen containing precursors without surface-passivation [14]. As, Amino acids are inexpensive, abundant, biocompatible and environmental friendly, having both carboxyl and amino groups and may serve as an ideal molecular precursor for CDs. Cui et al. have prepared CDs using amino acid as a precursor under basic or acidic conditions through a microwave process [16], while Huang et al. recently reported microwave synthesis of N-CDs from histidine [14]. Generally, the electron-donating property of CDs was verified only under photo- and electronic induction. In contrast, Dou et al. found the electron transfer process from CDs to dissolved oxygen in concentrated NaOH without any external force by CL process [15], thus CDs take part in redox reactions to enhance CL process. Herein, one-pot easily attainable hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent N-CDs from histidine, in environmental friendly way, without the use of any toxic reagent is reported. Up to the best of our knowledge, there is no report published to date on the CL enhancement and mechanism of KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs system.

Gallic acid is an important polyphenol with significant applications, and has increasing interest as food preservative and antioxidant [17–19], in pharmaceutical industry [20], due to their biological properties, including antihistaminic, anti-inflammatory, antitumor activities, protecting against cardiovascular diseases and scavenging of free radicals [21]. Moreover, gallic acid has many other applications such as used for making dyes, inks, acting as anti-fungal, anti-viral, as reducing agent and anti-allergic possessing [22,23]. Additionally, it can be used as clarifying agent in wine industry, flavoring agent in foods, meat products and to treat albuminuria and diabetes [22]. However, gallic acid is often used as an indicator of adulteration of fruit juices and alcoholic beverages [24]. Indeed, the perseverance of gallic acid is of interest not only due to its importance in food, medicine and industrial products, but also due to its toxicity to animals, possibly because of its reactions with macromolecules, like DNA and proteoglycans [25]. As, many in vivo and in vitro studies in animals, human and cell culture have marked its subsequent actions [26]. Therefore, developing sensitive method for gallic acid is very essential. Similarly, pyrogallol is a vital polyphenol with stout reducing properties and has been widely used as an antioxidant, scavenger of free radicals and reactive oxygen species [27]. On the other hand, pyrogallol has been widely used in many industries, including pharmaceuticals, plastics and cosmetics, acting as an antiseptic and dying material. Ever since, an active ingredient in industry, pyrogallol is usually found in industrial wastes. It is very essential to develop a sensitive method for pyrogallol monitoring because of concern of its inherent toxicity and environmental threat [20]. Thus the fortitude of pyrogallol is very important in chemical, environmental, clinical and biological systems. In summary, due to importance of gallic acid and pyrogallol, a need is felt for a better analytical method for its determination with high sensitivity. Based on this, a rapid, simple and direct detection method was proposed for gallic acid and pyrogallol determination. Moreover, this CL system showed virtuous performances such as high sensitivity and reproducibility, wide linear range and short analysis time.

Analytical grade chemicals were used during our work.HCl were obtained from Tianjin Kaitong Chemicals Co. (Tianjin, China) and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-4-piperidine (TEMP) from SigmaAldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Whereas, thiourea, NaN3, H2O2, ascorbic acid and NaOH were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. (Beijing, China), nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) from Nacalai Tesque Inc. (Tokyo, Japan) and DMPO (5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide) from Tokyo Kasei Kogyo Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). In addition, histidine was purchased from Beijing Dingguochangsheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. and KIO4 from Beijing Zhonglian Chemical Reagents (Beijing, China).The solutions were prepared in distilled water and stored at 4°C.

The CL was carried out with a BPCL Luminescence analyzer (Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing, China). Similarly, F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was arrayed for the photoluminescence and UV-3900s Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) for absorption studies. In addition, N-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 20S-Twin, FEI Company, USA) at 200kV. While, Bruker spectrometer (ESP-300E, Bruker, Germany) was deployed for EPR analysis.

A commercial autoclave reactor (30mL with Teflon lining and having stainless steel sheet) was used to prepare N-CDs. For this purpose 20mL of 0.1M solution i.e. a total of 3.1g of histidine was dissolved into 20 mL distilled water and transferred to 30mL Teflon lined autoclave. The reaction vessel was maintained at 240°C for 8h and then cooled to room temperature. As a result, brownish black solution was obtained which was filtered from 0.22µm membrane and used as such for CL studies without any further processes.

Indeed, a nitrogen-rich amino acid, histidine was used as a lone source to synthesize N-CDs through one-pot hydrothermal method. The syntheses was carried out, without the involvement of salt, base, acid, organic solvent or any other reagent, in an environmental friendly way. In this process, the high temperature (240°C) and pressure supplied energy by hydrothermal route, enabling dehydration and carbonization through reaction between the amino and carboxyl groups of histidine. As a result, the prepared N-CDs possess excellent solubility in water without any surface passivation, which may instigate from –COOH, –OH and –NH3+ groups on its surface [14]. Furthermore, HR-TEM studies showed that the average size of the N-CDs were 5nm ( Fig. 1). Moreover, the concentration of N-CDs solution was calculated according to the concentration of precursor solution.

The prepared N-CDs displayed excitation dependent PL emission, with a red shift from 340–440nm, which was imaginably accredited to the distribution of different sizes, emissive traps on its surface [15] and electronic conjugate structures [14]. A maximum emission at 467nm was observed in the PL spectrum when the N-CDs were excited at 366nm ( Fig. 2). In fact, the strong PL of N-CDs may be largely attributed to the quantum confinement of the passivated surface energy traps. In which, the isolated sp2 carbon dispersed within a sp3 carbon matrix acted as PL centers. In summary, the PL mechanism could result from the photogeneration of electron-hole pairs leading to radiative recombination of trap carriers localized within sp2 sites surrounded by sp3 defects. Especially, this mechanism could be effective in the incidence of heteroatoms [14]. Moreover, as presented in inset of Fig. S1, the as prepared N-CDs showed a blue color under a hand-held UV lamp, which was strong enough to be easily seen with bare eyes. On the other hand, the characteristic absorption bands at 295 and 270nm [14,15] could be credited to π–π* and n–π* electron transitions respectively (Fig. S1), in UV–visible absorption spectroscopy.

In CL study, the reaction of KIO4 with H2O2 resulted in weak but fast CL performance. For this purpose, when 50µL of 0.01M KIO4 was added to 50µL of 0.1M H2O2, the CL intensity reaches up to a maximum of 6000 in 0.2s and beyond that it decreased slowly (Fig. S2). Whereas, the addition of 50µL N-CDs resulted in 12 time increase in CL aptitude. In addition to that, acid and base in the presence of N-CDs caused 2 time decrease and 38 time increase respectively in CL intensity of the original reaction ( Fig. 3). In the attendance of acids the lower CL intensity may be attributed to the reduction of KIO4, which diminished its contribution in reaction. On the other hand, in basic medium the stability of different radicals accounts for the enhancement of CL signals.

In order to explore the effect of order of reagent on the CL intensity, the injection of different reagents were done ( Fig. 4). Firstly, H2O2 was injected to the mixture of KIO4–N-CDs, which resulted in very little increment in CL signals. The slight acidic nature of H2O2 [28], and no proper catalysis may be the reasons for its slight lower CL in this case. In second attempt N-CDs were injected to the mixture of KIO4–H2O2 solution in cuvette. So, its intensity was a bit higher than the first case, but lower from the last case, may be due to the loss of some 1O2 species before the addition of N-CDs. The highest intensity was observed when KIO4 were injected to the mixture of N-CDs and H2O2, due to the decomposition of H2O2 on the surface of N-CDs as well as to the energy transfer from 1O2 as emissive specie.

Similarly, the effect of cation was also studied by using the sodium and potassium salts of IO4 −, as well as the base with different cations (K+ and Na+), on the CL intensity (Fig. S3). Since, no effect was observed by the use of different cations. So, we neglected the role of these cations in the enhancement of the system.

A general approach to enhance CL emission comprises the increased production of free radicals, such as substrate radicals, •O2 −, •OH and 1O2 [29], engendered from the oxidation of reducing agents [8]. In order to detect these species, the effect of different free radical scavenger on CL intensity of the reaction with and without the presence of N-CDs and NaOH were carried out. The results are presented in Fig. S4 and Fig. S5 and summarized in Table 1.

The smaller decrease in CL intensity by addition of DMPO, in the presence of N-CDs only (11% decrease) and with NaOH (16% decrease), pointed the abundance of •O2 − and •OH radicals in the system. Although, DMPO could react with •O2 −, •OH and HO2 •, but it predominantly react with •O2 − above 7.7pH [15].

In order to know the effect of only •O2 − on the CL intensity, we checked the upshot of NBT (0.02M) with and without N-CDs and NaOH. Since, we observed a profound decrease (nearly 90 and 80% decrease) in all combinations. In fact, the reaction of NBT with •O2 − (Reaction 1) is more proficient (k=5.8×104 M−1 s−1) than DMPO (k=2.4M−1 s−1) in basic medium [15], that is why the effect in case of NBT was more reflective than DMPO. In summary, we concluded that in the presence of N-CDs, •O2 − was the most critical radical in the system [2,30, 26].
(1)
N
B
T
2 +
+
O

−
•

N

T

O
2
⁢

In particular, NaN3 was used to scavenge 1O2 (Reaction (2)) in the CL system [13,26]. The CL intensity of KIO4–H2O2 with and without N-CDs and NaOH were effectively quenched by 0.1M NaN3, indicated the production of 1O2. Therefore, it is clear from the free radical scavenging studies, that 1O2 played a critical role, produced by the reaction of periodate with the generated ROS (e.g. •O2 − or HO2 −) [31] as well as by the combination of free radicals in the system [13,26].
(2)
N
3
−
+
O
2
1
→
N
3
−
+
O
2
3

It was suspected that •OH played an important role in CL of KIO4–H2O2 system with and without the presence of N-CDs and NaOH. Which was checked by the addition of thiourea (0.1M), as an effective •OH radical scavenger [26,30]. Since, the effectively inhibition of CL intensities, confirmed the generation of •OH in the system.

Generally, ascorbic acid as a classical reducing agent, dehydrogenizes by ROS to form dehydroascorbic acid and is mostly used as a common free radical scavenger [26,30]. In fact, 50µL of 0.1M ascorbic acid almost completely repressed the CL intensity of all the four combinations. Therefore, it was confirmed that the CL reaction of KIO4–H2O2 in the presence and absence of N-CDs and NaOH ensued via radical pathway.

In summary, free radical scavenger studies suggested that CL originated in the presence and absence of N-CDs resulted in •O2 −, •OH and 1O2 species which were critical for CL boosting [8]. Additionally, from the results of ascorbic acid, thiourea and NBT, we concluded that CL of KIO4–H2O2 in presence and absence of N-CDs and NaOH proceeds via radical pathway.

Room temperature EPR analysis were carried out to detect radical intermediates generated in the system ( Fig. 5). TEMP (6,6-tetra- methyl 1-4-piperidine) as a specific target for 1O2, react with it to give TEMPO (2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidine-N-oxide), with a distinctive spectrum [13,26]. In this study, the observed signals of TEMPO supported the existence of 1O2 in KIO4–H2O2, KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs–NaOH and KIO4–N-CDs combinations (Fig. 5A). In contrast, no signals for KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs, indicating the absence of 1O2 in this case.

Moreover, DMPO (5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxisde), was used to detect the production of DMPO/•OH/•O2 − adducts in the studied system (Fig. 5B) [13,26,30]. The generation of •OH/•O2 − were confirmed in KIO4–H2O2, KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs and KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs–NaOH mixtures. Whereas, no such radicals were generated in KIO4–N-CDs system as it is clear from its spectrum. The absence of these radicals, in this case may be due to their production or stabilization of these radicals on the surface of N-CDs. Therefore, these radicals were unavailable to bind with DMPO to give their characteristic band in EPR spectrum.

To determine the emitive specie in KIO4–H2O2 in the presence and absence of N-CDs and NaOH, CL emission spectra were obtained on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), with a flow cell, without light source. Different strategies were adopted to know the emitting specie in KIO4–H2O2 system in the presence of N-CDs at different pH. In case of KIO4–H2O2 system, the CL signals were attributed to emission from 1O2 in light of EPR, free radicals scavenger studies and CL spectra. As, it is believed that CL emission in KIO4–H2O2 was from 1O2 and an excited oxygen molecular pair O2(1Δg)O2(1Σ− g). In fact, 1O2 was much more easily formed and was the only emitting specie in this combination [5]. Therefore, the wide CL emission in KIO4–H2O2 spectrum ( Fig. 6a) may be ascribed to excited 1O2 or excited oxygen dimol specie (O2)2*.

However, in the presence of N-CDs (Fig. 6b) the emission was from N-CDs along with 1O2, as the emission peak was in ample match with the PL spectrum (Fig. 2) of N-CDs. In this case, the EPR results (Fig. 5A) designated the absence of 1O2, but we cannot neglect the contribution of 1O2 due to constrain effect of NaN3 (Table 1). The presence of 1O2 was only traced by free radical scavenger studies. These results compel us to propose that 1O2 may be produced on the surface of N-CDs. Therefore, assessment of 1O2 cannot be made in a straight forward application of the well-known EPR analysis.
On the other hand, in basic medium (Fig. 6c) high emission near to 490nm indicated the emission from 1O2 along with N-CDs which was also supported by EPR results (Fig. 5A).

We performed a detailed mechanistic study of N-CDs enhanced KIO4–H2O2 system to identify the role of N-CDs by using different experiments. As, the CL analysis could be used to assure the occurrence of a redox reaction [15]. Generally, CL bands originated when IO4 − react with H2O2 in acidic, neutral and basic mediums to give IO3 − and 1O2 [3], following different routes of production and utilization of •O2 −, •OH and 1O2 [29]. In our system, the different combinations of reagents with their proposed mechanism are discussed below in detail.

A weak CL was reported by the addition of KIO4 solution to H2O2. In this process, KIO4 reacted with H2O2 to produce •O2 − radical (Reaction (3)), which upon combination generate 1O2, thus emit with maximum wavelength of 478nm from 1O2 and excited oxygen molecular pairs [5,11].
(3)

Additionally, •O2 − and •OH radicals could also be generated from the reaction of IO4 − with dissolved O2 (Reaction (4)) or by Reaction (5) [31]. The recombination of these reactive oxygen species generated 1O2 (Reactions (6)–(8)) [4,13,15, 31,5].

Similarly, the IO4 − specie reacts with the free radicals generated in the system to produce 1O2 (Reaction (9)) [31]. As, it is also clear from the EPR studies of KIO4–H2O2 system, that •OH and •O2 − were produced (Fig. 5B), which resulted in the production of 1O2 (Fig. 5A) [31].

Therefore, the excited oxygen molecular pair O2(1Δg)O2(1Σ− g) and 1O2 were the principals emitive specie in KIO4–H2O2 reaction (Reactions (10) and (11)) [5].

The CL enhancement by molecularly imprinted polymer capped Mn-doped ZnS QDs of H2O2–IO4 − reaction was attributed to emission from QDs [1]. In strong basic condition, Dou et al. also credited the only one maximum emission peak to the emission of CDs, which was same with the FL spectra of CDs rather than the emission band of dimol singlet oxygen (1O2)2 (381, 477 and 578nm) [15]. In our case, the weak CL from H2O2–IO4 − system was enhanced by the application of N-CDs,which acting as a catalyst, energy acceptor as well as redox specie in this system.
Firstly, in KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs system, N-CDs catalyzed the decomposition of H2O2 to generate •OH and •O2 −, resulted in the generation of 1O2 on its surface from the recombination of these radicals.

Secondly, the produced •O2 − ion inject electrons in the conduction band of N-CDs (Reaction (12)), while •OH inject holes in the valence band of N-CDs (Reaction (13)), to form positively and negatively charged N-CDs, which upon recombination give excited N-CDs* (Reaction (14)).
(12)
N C D +
O
2
−
•
→ N C
D
• −
+
O
2
(13)
N C D +
O
•
H → N C
D
• +
+
O
−
H
(14)
N C
D
• −
+ N C
D
• +
→ N C D * + N C D

Additionally, the N-CD•− thus produced can also be excited by reacting with oxidizing radicals or reagents in the system (Reactions (15) and (16)) [26].
(15)
N C
D
• −
+
O
•
H → N C D * +
O
−
H
(16)
N C
D
• −
+
H
2
O
2
→ N C D * + 2
O
−
H

Similarly, the produced N-CD•+ can also be excited by reacting with radicals like •O2 − (Reaction (17)) [26].
(17)
N C
D
• +
+
O
2
−
•
→ N C D * +
O
2

Lastly, the other emissive species of KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs system i.e. 1O2 and excited oxygen molecular pairs O2(1Δg)O2(1Σ− g), transfer its energy to N-CDs, and thus, the excited N-CDs* generated in the system. Unfortunately, there were no signals in EPR spectrum for singlet oxygen in this combination (Fig. 5A). However the constrain effect of NaN3 indicated the production of 1O2 (Table 1). In fact, the generation of 1O2 on the surface of N-CDs may be the reason for no signals in EPR studies. The CL resonance energy transfer process may occur between the donors 1O2 and the acceptors N-CDs to from excited N-CDs*. The amino and carboxyl group at the surface [13] of N-CDs behaved as the receptor sites and was very significant in the CL energy transfer process. 1O2 was also active and easily form excited (1O2)2 (Reaction (18)), which easily transfer its energy [13] to N-CDs forming excited N-CDs* [15].
(18)
O
2
1
+
O
2
1
→
(
O
2
1
)
2
*

To check the oxidation of different groups attached on the surface of N-CDs, we did the EPR study. In KIO4–N-CDs combination, no free radicals were detected (Fig. 5B), but the presence of 1O2 was noticed (Fig. 5A). This may be due to production and stabilization of free radicals on the surface of N-CDs. Especially, the formation of 1O2 in the reaction of KIO4 with N-CDs follows the pathway of reactions (19) and (20). Indeed, the hydroxyl and amine groups on the surface of N-CDs may be oxidized to form 1O2 [5].
(19)
I
O
4
−
+ N C D →
P
/
+ I
O
3
−
(20)
P
/
→
O 2
1
+ N C D

[5] All these practices lead to the generation of same N-CD*.The excited N-CDs generated by all the above ways, resulted in enhancement of CL signals by returning to ground state (Reaction (21)).
(21)
N C D * → N C D + h v

Thus the enhancement of N-CDs was assumed to catalytic process, to accelerate the electron transfer process and to facilitate radical generation by decomposing H2O2, redox reaction of N-CDs, as well as to energy acceptor through CRET from 1O2 and (O2)2*.

The addition of base to KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs further boosted the CL signals. The high stability of •O2 − in basic medium (decay rate constant k=2M−1 s−1), compared to neutral or acidic mediums (k=8.5×107 M−1 s−1) may be one of the reason which further provoked CL intensity [15]. In basic medium, KIO4 interacted with H2O2 producing •OH and •O2 − radicals [1,13]. As explained in the previous section, the generation of ROS such as •O2 − and •OH could increase the CL intensity by redox reactions with N-CDs [31]. Furthermore, in KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs–NaOH system the generation of •OH and •O2 − (Fig. 5B) and 1O2 (Fig. 5A) were also confirmed by EPR studies. The second explanation for the noticeable increase in CL intensity at high pH could be given to the possibility that −OH either participated or played a key role in CL reaction [29].

The production of •O2 − is suppressed by acids i.e. the radicals were destabilized and secondly the IO4 − may be reduced at lower pH (Reaction (22)) [2]. Thus the combined effect of both caused two times decrease in CL intensity of KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs–HCl to that of KIO4–H2O2 system and 35 time decrease compared to KIO4–H2O2–N-CDs system (Fig. 3) [2].
(22)
I
O
4
−
+ 2
e
−
+ 2
H
+
⇌ I
O
3
−
+
H
2
O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curves and detection limit of CL oxidation of pyrogallol and gallic acid
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CDs enhanced CL system was successfully used for the determination of pyrogallol and gallic acid. The acidity of solution greatly influences the electrochemical activity of pyrogallol. Pyrogallol was auto-oxidized in basic medium, but in acidic solution shows good electrochemical bustle [27]. Similarly, gallic acid being a weak acid with a primary ionization constant of 4.63×10−6, which is suppressed in acidic medium, while in alkaline medium have larger magnitude [17,32]. Therefore, gallic acid are stable when hydrolyzed with acid [22,23], and the acidified solution prevents its ionization [18]. So the oxidation of gallic acid and pyrogallol was limited to lower pH range, while above 7.5pH, the oxidation almost disappeared [17,33]. In neutral medium the system senses these compounds to a higher concentration of 10−3 M and was senseless to low concentration. Thus the acidic environment was selected for gallic acid and pyrogallol determination [19]. Therefore, the solutions of pyrogallol and gallic acid were prepared in 0.01M HCl to avoid the auto-oxidation as well as to increase the sensitivity of the system for these compounds. The oxidation of pyrogallol and gallic acid by •O2 − generated aryloxy radicals from phenolic hydroxyl and can be stabilized via intramolecular hydrogen bonds by ortho- hydroxyl groups (resonates to different structure) ( Fig. 7) [11]. The •O2 − are competitively consumed by these compounds leading to inhibition of CL intensity. The CL intensity linearly decreases with the increase of pyrogallol and gallic acid concentration in the range of 1.0×10−4–1.0×10−7 M. In addition, the limits of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were deliberated using the statistical relations (sd/m) 3.3 and (sd/m) 10, respectively. Whereas, sd is the standard deviation for an arithmetic average of 11 readings at the same conditions and m is in the slope of the calibration curve [33]. The LOD was found to be 4.6×10−8 and 6.1×10−8 M for pyrogallol and gallic acid respectively. Moreover, the Pearson's correlation coefficient (R) was 0.996 for pyrogallol and 0.993 for gallic acid. Whereas, the RSD for gallic acid and pyrogallol was 1.4% and 2.3% respectively (n=11). The results are summarized in Table 2 and presented in Fig. 8.

The aim of this work was to develop a new sensitized CL to broaden the analytical application of KIO4–H2O2 system. Indeed, the weak CL of KIO4–H2O2 were dramatically enhanced by N-CDs, acted as an efficient catalyst and an energy acceptor by CRET, which was further boosted by basic medium. The emitting species in KIO4–H2O2 system was 1O2, while N-CDs combined it as an emitting sort in it presence. On mechanistic ground, the decomposition of H2O2 and the formation of 1O2 and excited (1O2)2 were promoted on the surface of N-CDs. Additionally, the energy transfer and the contribution of redox reaction of N-CDs, to form excited N-CDs*, also subsidized the enhancement of CL signals. On the other hand,the noticeable increase in CL intensity at high pH could be attributed to the possibility that −OH either participated or played a key role in CL reaction and to the stability of generated radicals. In contrast, the constrain effect of acid on the CL intensity was due to oxidation of KIO4. Furthermore, in light of our results, we proposed that the CL reaction of KIO4–H2O2 in the presence and absence of N-CDs and NaOH proceeds via radical pathway.

The enhanced system was used to sense pyrogallol and gallic acid with a low detection limit of 10−8 M having a linear range of 1.0×10−4–1.0×10−7 M. Additionally, we found that at low concentration in aqueous medium, pyrogallol and gallic acid were auto-oxidized, which was decreased by the use of acidic solution. The attained results unveil that the present method is more sensitive, faster, simpler and less costly compared to other methods, and could be applied to determine polyphenols in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81373373, 91213305 and 21227006). In addition, S.N.A Shah highly acknowledges the Chinese Scholarship Council for the award of doctoral studies.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.talanta.2016.02.056.
